figures.caption: Figure 9: Analysis of y-forces prediction errors for the 2-D example. (a) Histogram of scenario-based relative L2 errors across all test scenarios. (b-g) Parity plots comparing predicted and ground-truth values for the three best-performing scenarios (middle row) and the three worst-performing scenarios (bottom row), where the dashed diagonal lines indicate ideal agreement. Note: Scenarios where the wind directions are aligned with the x-axis (angle = 0◦) are excluded as the near-zero y-force values are dominated by numerical noise.
Table 4: Error metrics of the three worst-performing scenarios for each variable in the 2-D example, identified by the three highest RLE(s) magnitudes across all test cases.
Figure 2: Schematic diagram of the 1-D example. The initial wave starts at x = 0 and propagates along the positive x-axis.
Figure 6: Bathymetry field of the 2-D example. The initial wave starts at the left boundary (x = 0) and propagates towards the shore along the right boundary (x = 38 km).

Figure 12: Analysis of Hsig prediction errors for the DUCK test example. (a) Histogram of scenario-based relative L2 errors across all test scenarios. (b-g) Parity plots comparing predicted and ground-truth values for the three best-performing scenarios (middle row) and the three worst-performing scenarios (bottom row), where the dashed diagonal lines indicate ideal agreement.

Figure 11: The left figure shows the overlap of the computational grid centers (grey) with the bathymetry (color). The computational grid extends beyond the surveyed bathymatry to minimize boundary effects. After SWAN is run, it generates the x, y coordinates along with their corresponding water depths, Hsig, and other target wave properties. The right figure illustrates the spatial distribution of water depth.

Table 7: Configurations of the three worst-performing scenarios for each variable in DUCK example, as reported in Table 6. Wind speed is given in m/s, wave height in m, and directional quantities in degrees.

Figure 1: Conceptual overview of the DeepONet architecture for wave modeling. For each wave–wind scenario, boundary wave information and wind forcing are encoded by the branch network, while spatial coordinates are provided to the trunk network. The learned operator maps scenario-level forcing to spatially resolved wave fields, approximating the SWAN solution operator. Ground-truth wave fields from SWAN are used to supervise training through a point-wise loss function.
Table 6: Error metrics of the three worst-performing scenarios for each variable in the Duck example, identified by the three highest RLE(s) magnitudes across all test cases.

Figure 15: Comparison of the worst-case scenarios for each variable between SWAN and DeepONet in DUCK example. Wind and wave settings are specified in Table 7. The top row (a–c) shows the ground truth, DeepONet prediction, and relative L2 error for the worst Hsig scenario. The middle row (d–f) and bottom row (g–i) present the ground truth, DeepONet prediction, and relative L2 error for the worst x-force and y-force predictions, respectively.

Figure 4: Analysis of Forces prediction errors for the 1-D example. (a) Histogram of scenario-based relative L2 errors across all test scenarios. (b-g) Parity plots comparing predicted and ground-truth values for the three best-performing scenarios (middle row) and the three worst-performing scenarios (bottom row), where the dashed diagonal lines indicate ideal agreement.

Figure 7: Analysis of Hsig prediction errors for the 2-D example. (a) Histogram of scenariobased relative L2 errors across all test scenarios. (b-g) Parity plots comparing predicted and ground-truth values for the three best-performing scenarios (middle row) and the three worst-performing scenarios (bottom row), where the dashed diagonal lines indicate ideal agreement.

Figure 10: Comparison of the worst-case scenarios for each variable between SWAN and DeepONet in 2-D example. Wind and wave settings are specified in Table 5. The top row (a–c) shows the ground truth, DeepONet prediction, and node-wise relative L2 error for the worst Hsig scenario, 2H1. The middle row (d–f) and bottom row (g–i) present the ground truth, DeepONet prediction, and node-wise relative L2 error for the worst x-force and y-force scenarios (2X1 and 2Y1), respectively.

Table 1: The best-performing set of hyperparameters obtained from Optuna. ∗ A larger batch size (256) was used for the DUCK case to reduce computational overhead, as smaller batch sizes resulted in excessive training cost.
Figure 16: Location of the maximum absolute error of each scenario across all test scenarios
Table 3: Configurations of the three worst-performing scenarios reported in Table 2. Wind speed is given in m/s, wave height in m, and directional quantities in degrees.

Figure 3: Analysis of Hsig prediction errors for the 1-D example. (a) Histogram of scenariobased relative L2 errors across all test scenarios. Figure 5: Comparison between SWAN and DeepONet for the worst-case scenarios in the 1-D example. The top panel shows the Hsig prediction and the bottom panel shows the wave-induced forces prediction.
